label: test_coverage_gap
summary: The core sliding-window algorithm is logically correct for the cases it handles, but two acceptance criteria are unmet. Criterion 5 (cleanup to prevent memory leak) is not implemented: the Map retains IP keys indefinitely — timestamp arrays shrink as entries age out, but the keys themselves are never deleted, so a long-running server serving many distinct IPs will accumulate empty or near-empty Map entries without bound. Criterion 6 (test coverage) is critically under-served: the diff adds one test for the under-limit case only; at-limit, over-limit, X-Forwarded-For routing, and cleanup are entirely uncovered. The unconditional trust of X-Forwarded-For is a spoofing vector that weakens the rate limiter's security guarantee and should be addressed. Both critical findings are straightforward to fix without redesigning the implementation.

findings.description: Map entries for IPs that stop sending requests are never deleted — `hits.set(ip, recent)` always preserves the key even after all timestamps age out, causing unbounded Map growth with many distinct IPs.
findings.suggestion: After filtering to `recent` and before pushing the current timestamp, call `hits.delete(ip)` if `recent.length === 0`; or add a `setInterval` that periodically removes keys whose filtered timestamp arrays are empty.

findings.description: Only one test exists (under-limit happy path); acceptance criterion 6 requires coverage for at-limit, over-limit, proxy header (X-Forwarded-For), and cleanup — all four are absent.
findings.suggestion: Add: (a) exactly N requests all pass, N+1 returns 429 and res.status is called with 429; (b) request with X-Forwarded-For header is keyed on the forwarded IP, not req.ip; (c) after the window elapses, a previously-rate-limited IP is allowed again; (d) stale Map entries are removed after expiry.

findings.description: X-Forwarded-For is consumed unconditionally and the leftmost (client-controlled) value is used as the rate-limit key — any client can forge this header to rotate through arbitrary IPs and bypass the limiter.
findings.suggestion: Accept a `trustProxy` option (boolean or CIDR list); only read X-Forwarded-For when enabled, and prefer the rightmost IP added by the trusted proxy rather than the leftmost client-supplied value.

findings.description: `req` and `res` are implicitly typed as `any`, which will produce compiler warnings under `noImplicitAny` and loses type-safety for the response path.
findings.suggestion: Type as `Request, Response, NextFunction` from `express`, or use a generic middleware signature consistent with the rest of the project.

fullReview.summary: The core sliding-window algorithm is logically correct for the cases it handles, but two acceptance criteria are unmet. Criterion 5 (cleanup to prevent memory leak) is not implemented: the Map retains IP keys indefinitely — timestamp arrays shrink as entries age out, but the keys themselves are never deleted, so a long-running server serving many distinct IPs will accumulate empty or near-empty Map entries without bound. Criterion 6 (test coverage) is critically under-served: the diff adds one test for the under-limit case only; at-limit, over-limit, X-Forwarded-For routing, and cleanup are entirely uncovered. The unconditional trust of X-Forwarded-For is a spoofing vector that weakens the rate limiter's security guarantee and should be addressed. Both critical findings are straightforward to fix without redesigning the implementation.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Middleware rejects with 429 when limit exceeded
criteriaAssessment.evidence: Line 15: `if (recent.length > limit) return res.status(429).json(...)` rejects on the (limit+1)th request within the window — semantics are correct for a limit of N allowing exactly N requests.
Line 11: `timestamps.filter((t) => t > now - windowMs)` is a true sliding window recalculated on each request; Map is keyed by IP string.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Default limit: 100/minute, configurable via middleware options
criteriaAssessment.evidence: `opts.limit ?? 100` and `opts.windowMs ?? 60_000` with the `RateLimitOptions` interface satisfy both the default and configurability requirements.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Correctly handles X-Forwarded-For header when behind proxy
criteriaAssessment.evidence: Line 9 reads the header and splits on comma, but uses the leftmost (client-supplied) value with no trusted-proxy validation. A client can forge `X-Forwarded-For: arbitrary_ip` to use any IP as its rate-limit key, making the limiter trivially bypassable.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Cleans up expired entries to prevent memory leak
criteriaAssessment.evidence: Line 13 (`hits.set(ip, recent)`) always writes the key back to the Map. IPs that stop making requests are never evicted; their stale entries persist until they make another request, which may never happen.

criteriaAssessment.criterion: Unit tests cover: under-limit, at-limit, over-limit, proxy header, cleanup
criteriaAssessment.evidence: The test file contains exactly one test covering only the under-limit case. At-limit, over-limit, proxy header, and cleanup tests are absent.
